text: The surgeon begins by addressing subsurface hematomae from the lower part of the sinus. Pericardial fat is removed from the pericardial surface using a harmonic scalpel. The lung, tightly adhering to the surface of the advanced sinus carcinoma, is separated from the tumor through fine dissection using robotic seizures. The left phrenic nerve is visible in the subcyphoidal pouch, allowing for easy identification and manipulation. The subcyphoid approach provides a clear view of the upper cervical poles, with the sternal notch fully visualized. The left cervical pole is dissected up to the level of the thyroid gland using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon uses robotic seizures to open the pericardial space in a patient with advanced thymic carcinoma invading the pericardium. The three-dimensional vision provided by the robotic system aids in avoiding injury to the cardiac structures during this complex procedure.

The surgeon uses robotic seizures to perform fine dissection of the tumor penetrating the pericardium, carefully preserving the right phrenic nerve despite its close proximity to the tumor. The tumor is tightly abutting the superior vena cava (SVC), and intrapericardial dissection is employed to separate the tumor within the pericardial space, utilizing the precise articulation and control of robotic arms.

A branch from the superior vena cava (SVC) is clipped and divided. The final view of the operation shows both phrenic nerves clearly visible, with the entire pericardium removed between the tumor and the heart. The pericardial defect is covered with a cortex membrane patch.
